abstract: A significant hurdle in electrochemical carbon dioxide reduction is developing catalytic materials that combine high product selectivity and long-term stability with a composition of earth-abundant elements. In this study, we present thin films of nanosized metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) as atomically defined, nanoscopic catalysts capable of efficiently reducing $\text{CO}_2$ to carbon monoxide in aqueous electrolytes. By optimizing the film thickness and composition through atomic layer deposition-mediated synthesis, we identified a cobalt-porphyrin MOF, $\text{Al}_2(\text{OH})_2\text{TCPP-Co}$, as a high-performance system. This material achieved a $\text{CO}$ selectivity exceeding 76% and sustained activity over seven hours with a per-site turnover number (TON) of 1400. In situ spectroelectrochemical analysis provided real-time insight into the cobalt oxidation states, demonstrating that the framework’s catalytic centers are redox-accessible and undergo a $\text{Co(II)}$ to $\text{Co(I)}$ reduction during the catalytic cycle. This work highlights the potential of MOFs as a modular platform for bridging the gap between molecular and heterogeneous electrocatalysis.
